title: Orf in sheep and goats: recognition and management
subtitle: The scabby mouth disease that spreads fast in a young flock.
excerpt: Orf is a viral skin disease that causes painful crusty lesions around the mouth, feet, and teats in lambs and kids. It spreads quickly within a group and can persist on the premises for years. Most animals recover in 2 to 4 weeks without treatment, but affected young animals may struggle to feed, and the virus transfers to humans on contact.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep (2023). AHDB Beef & Lamb orf factsheet, ahdb.org.uk. Public Health England (now UKHSA) guidance on zoonotic risks from orf, ukhsa.gov.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: A viral disease caused by Parapoxvirus ovis, producing crusty proliferative lesions on the lips, muzzle, gums, and feet of sheep and goats. Highly contagious between animals; also transmissible to humans through broken skin.

content.text: typically appears in lambs and kids in spring at or after their first handling or show event, or following any damage to the lips through abrasive pasture or hay feeding. Outbreaks in housed animals spread rapidly through direct contact and contaminated surfaces.
Small red papules (spots) on the lips and muzzle that progress to thick warty scabs over 5 to 10 days
Lesions on the feet (coronary band) causing lameness in severe cases
Ewe teat lesions causing mastitis if lambs transmit the virus through suckling
Reduced feeding in lambs, a lamb with severe mouth lesions may lose condition rapidly

Most animals recover without intervention in 2 to 4 weeks. Isolate severely affected animals where possible to limit spread. For lambs that are not feeding due to mouth pain, offer softened pellets or creep mixed with warm water to reduce the need to bite. Check ewes' teats daily, a secondary bacterial mastitis on an orf-infected teat can develop quickly and needs antibiotic treatment from a vet.

Orf transfers to humans through direct contact with lesions, especially through cuts or abrasions on the hands. Wear gloves when handling affected animals. Human orf produces a single raised lesion on the fingers or hands that resolves in 3 to 6 weeks without treatment. The risk is highest to people who are immunosuppressed.

An orf vaccine is available (live attenuated virus, scarified onto the inner thigh skin). Vaccinating on land that has no orf history introduces the virus permanently to the premises, as the vaccine strain persists in the soil. Vaccination is most appropriate on premises with a documented recurrent problem. Consult your vet before vaccinating for the first time.

The orf virus survives in dry soil, in building crevices, and on dried scabs for months to years. Once on a holding, it persists. Newly introduced naive animals (whether lambs or bought-in replacements) remain susceptible. Annual challenge in young stock is common on affected premises, and previously infected animals acquire partial immunity that reduces severity rather than preventing reinfection.
